# On-Chain Engineering Blog

> Technical blog by Ethernal covering EVM internals, EIP/ERC explainers, L2 rollup architecture, smart contract security, and block explorer development.

## Articles

- [How Three Infrastructure Failures Turned a $50M Collateral Swap into $36K](https://tryethernal.com/blog/50m-defi-routing-failure): A $50M Aave collateral swap returned $36K. Three compounding CoW Protocol failures made the worst possible route the only one available.
- [Best Block Explorers for EVM Chains in 2026](https://tryethernal.com/blog/best-block-explorers-evm-chains-2026): Compare EVM block explorers in 2026: Etherscan, Blockscout, Ethernal, Tenderly, Blockchair, Routescan. Setup speed, pricing, and a decision framework.
- [Data Retention Changes](https://tryethernal.com/blog/data-retention-changes): Data will now be cleared after 7 days for free plan users.
- [When Provers Race: The Coordination Problem in Decentralized ZK](https://tryethernal.com/blog/decentralized-zk-proving-coordination): Concurrent ZK provers collide at 33K gas per failure. Why the coordination problem is hard and what three proposed mechanisms look like.
- [One Signature, Every Chain: EIP-8130's Protocol-Level Account Abstraction](https://tryethernal.com/blog/eip-8130-protocol-level-account-abstraction): EIP-8130 proposes native AA with chainId=0 config sync. No bundlers, no per-chain key rotations. Here's how the mechanism actually works.
- [The Frame Wallet: What EIP-8141 Means for Every EOA](https://tryethernal.com/blog/eip-8141-frame-transactions-eoa-abstraction): EIP-8141 gives unmodified EOAs gas abstraction via default code and a new frame execution model. No bundlers, no migration, no new wallet.
- [Ethernal Is Going Open Source!](https://tryethernal.com/blog/ethernal-is-going-open-source): Ethernal's code is now publicly available on GitHub. The hosted version now has free and paid tiers.
- [Ethernal x Alchemy](https://tryethernal.com/blog/ethernal-x-alchemy): Add a webhook in your Alchemy dashboard and get a block explorer for your dapp. Automatic transaction syncing and decoded function calls.
- [Ethernal x Tenderly](https://tryethernal.com/blog/ethernal-x-tenderly): Set up a block explorer for your Tenderly forks. Debug transactions sent through Ethernal's UI directly in Tenderly.
- [EVM Opcodes 101: What Happens When You Send a Transaction](https://tryethernal.com/blog/evm-opcodes-101): EVM opcodes are the low-level instructions behind every Ethereum transaction. Learn how stack, memory, storage, and gas costs work.
- [Interact with Mainnet Contracts on Your Local Fork](https://tryethernal.com/blog/interact-with-deployed-contracts-etherscan-integration): Import contracts from Etherscan into your Ethernal dashboard to interact with them on your local mainnet fork.
- [Public EVM Block Explorers](https://tryethernal.com/blog/public-explorers): Spin up public block explorers for your EVM chains. Custom domains, contract verification, token transfers, and Metamask integration.
- [The Bug Your Auditor's Tools Will Never Catch](https://tryethernal.com/blog/the-bug-auditors-tools-miss): Static analysis and fuzzing miss a whole class of smart contract vulnerabilities. Here's what logical bugs look like and how to find them.
- [The Commerce Layer: How ERC-8183 Makes AI Agent Payments Verifiable](https://tryethernal.com/blog/the-commerce-layer-erc-8183): ERC-8183 adds the missing verb to agent commerce: not 'I sent value' but 'I will pay when you deliver.' How the job lifecycle and evaluator work.
- [The Shape-Shifting Agent: Mutable Configuration Is Ethereum's Next AI Security Problem](https://tryethernal.com/blog/the-shape-shifting-agent-erc-8172): ERC-8172 proposes block-delayed metadata updates for AI agents. Here's why the problem is harder than it looks, and what the full defense stack looks like.
- [Transaction Tracing with Ethernal](https://tryethernal.com/blog/transaction-tracing-with-ethernal): Easily see which contracts were called or created during the execution of a function. A deep dive into Ethernal's transaction tracing feature.
- [What Is a Layer 2, and Why Does Your Rollup Need a Block Explorer?](https://tryethernal.com/blog/what-is-l2-why-rollup-needs-block-explorer): L2 rollups process thousands of transactions off-chain, but without a block explorer, debugging and verification become guesswork.

## About

- [Ethernal](https://tryethernal.com): Open-source block explorer for EVM-based chains
- [GitHub](https://github.com/tryethernal/ethernal): Source code repository
- [Documentation](https://doc.tryethernal.com): Full product documentation
